did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically EfficientNetB0 and EfficientNetB3, to classify skin lesion images as benign or malignant for early skin cancer detection. It also compares these models against VGG16, Xception, and MobileNetV2 using metrics like accuracy and AUC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design, implementation, and validation of Convolutional Neural Networks (EfficientNetB0 and EfficientNetB3) for skin cancer detection. It includes specific sections on methodology (data pre-processing, model building), results (accuracy, AUC metrics, classification reports), and comparison with other models (VGG16, Xception, MobileNetV2). Although published in a conference (ICoICC), it reports new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing early detection of skin cancer: A convolutional neural network approach using EfficientNet models
journal: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent and Cloud Computing (ICoICC)
brief_summary: This study aims to enhance the early detection of skin cancer by classifying skin lesion images as benign or malignant using Convolutional Neural Networks, specifically EfficientNetB0 and EfficientNetB3. The models were trained on a public dataset sourced from ISIC Archive, which contained approximately 1,800 images divided into training and testing directories. Data preprocessing included resizing images to 224x224 pixels and applying augmentation techniques such as zooming, rotation, and horizontal flipping to increase dataset size and improve generalization. The models were evaluated using accuracy, precision, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics, with performance validated through K-Fold cross-validation (k=5 and k=10). EfficientNetB3 achieved the highest test accuracy of 87.01%, while EfficientNetB0 reached 82.68%, with K-Fold mean accuracies of 85.17% and 84.98% respectively for k=5. The results demonstrate that EfficientNet models outperform comparative models like VGG16, Xception, and MobileNetV2, making them effective tools for early skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification labels used in the experiment. The dataset contained sub-directories for benign and malignant images.
One of the two binary classification labels used in the experiment. The study aims to detect skin cancer by classifying lesions as malignant.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify the cancer type as benign or malignant' using EfficientNetB0 and EfficientNetB3 models. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories from skin lesion images. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, and AUC, with classification reports provided for both models.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images sourced from https://www.isic-archive.com/. It was organized into training and testing directories, each containing sub-directories for benign and malignant images.

use.training: The original dataset was split into training and testing directories. The training directory images were augmented (zooming, rotation, horizontal flipping) to increase dataset size for model training.
use.validation: The test directory images were further divided, with 30% of the original test directory images allocated to a validation directory.
use.testing: The remaining 70% of the original test directory images were used for the test directory to evaluate model performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance and assess generalization by dividing the dataset into k groups (k=5 and k=10), using one group as validation/test and the rest for training, then taking the mean of performance metrics.
To visualize and evaluate the diagnostic ability of the binary classifier systems (EfficientNetB0 and EfficientNetB3) at various threshold settings.
To quantify the overall performance of the classification models, representing the degree of separability between benign and malignant classes.
To provide a detailed breakdown of model performance metrics for each class (benign and malignant) and macro/weighted averages.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, AUC, and K-Fold cross-validation mean metrics without statistical significance testing.

EfficientNetB0 Classification Report.notes: Classification report provided for EfficientNetB0 on test data.

EfficientNetB3 Classification Report.notes: Classification report provided for EfficientNetB3 on test data.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using weights from ImageNet

architecture_details.base_model_modification: The fully connected layer at the top of the base model was excluded

custom_layers.description: Calculates the average value of the input tensor feature map to produce a smaller tensor

training_details.early_stopping: Model was saved when validation accuracy was at its maximum
training_details.validation_strategy: K-Fold Cross Validation with k=5 and k=10

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via shifting height and width
Filling empty areas with nearest pixels of original images
conclusions: EfficientNetB3 achieved the highest test accuracy of 87.01% and an AUC score of approximately 0.88, outperforming EfficientNetB0 (82.68% accuracy, 0.83 AUC), VGG16 (80.74% accuracy), Xception (83.77% accuracy), and MobileNetV2 (45.45% accuracy).

EfficientNetB0 achieved a mean accuracy of 85.40% and EfficientNetB3 achieved 85.25% with k=10 K-Fold cross-validation, while with k=5, EfficientNetB0 reached 84.98% and EfficientNetB3 reached 85.17%.

The proposed CNN models, specifically EfficientNetB0 and EfficientNetB3, demonstrate high accuracy and effectiveness as tools for the early diagnosis of skin cancer by classifying lesions as benign or malignant.
Data augmentation techniques (zooming, rotation, horizontal flipping, shifting) and transfer learning from ImageNet were effective in improving model prediction and reducing overfitting.
Future work aims to enhance these methods for real-time mobile use and to combine multiple data types for improved diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small public dataset of approximately 1,800 images, which limits the generalizability and robustness of the findings.

The dataset consists of only two classes (benign vs. malignant), ignoring the clinical reality of multiple skin cancer subtypes (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and benign conditions.

There is no external validation; the models were evaluated solely on the same public dataset (ISIC Archive) used for training, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of generalization to diverse clinical populations.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the performance differences between EfficientNet models and comparators like VGG16, Xception, and MobileNetV2.
MobileNetV2 performed poorly (45.45% accuracy), which is unusually low for this architecture on similar tasks, suggesting potential implementation issues or dataset bias that warrants investigation.

The authors acknowledge that the pre-processed images often lacked visually detectable objects, which may reduce the model's efficiency in real-world clinical settings where such artifacts are common.

Data leakage risks are present as the paper does not explicitly detail how the split between training, validation, and testing sets was performed to ensure no patient overlap, which is critical in medical imaging datasets.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICoICC) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
